title: Lean FIRE Annual Expenses: My Real $1,800/Month Life in Vietnam
subtitle: My actual numbers, what I gave up, what I got back, and how to calculate yours
description: My real Lean FIRE annual expenses: $1,800/month ($21,600/year) living in Vietnam. Rent $375, food $600, visa $100. That's a $540,000 FIRE number. Here's the honest breakdown — what I sacrifice and what I don't.

faqs.question: What is the typical Lean FIRE annual spending amount?
faqs.answer: Lean FIRE annual expenses typically run $20,000–$40,000/year for a single person in the US. Internationally, particularly in Southeast Asia, you can sustain a full lifestyle on $18,000–$25,000/year. A real example: $1,800/month ($21,600/year) in Vietnam covers rent, food, visa costs, and travel.

faqs.question: What does Lean FIRE mean?
faqs.answer: Lean FIRE (Financial Independence, Retire Early) is early retirement on a minimal but intentional budget — typically $20,000–$40,000/year for a single person. It requires a much smaller portfolio than traditional FIRE by keeping annual expenses low, usually through geographic arbitrage, housing optimisation, or both.

faqs.question: How do you calculate your Lean FIRE number?
faqs.answer: Divide your annual expenses by your target withdrawal rate. At 4%, multiply annual expenses by 25. At a safer 3.25%, multiply by 31. For example, $21,600/year × 25 = $540,000 at 4%, or $669,600 at 3.25%.

faqs.question: What are typical Lean FIRE annual expenses?
faqs.answer: Typical Lean FIRE annual expenses range from $18,000 to $40,000 per year depending on location and lifestyle. In Southeast Asia or LCOL countries, $20,000–$25,000/year is achievable. In the US, $25,000–$40,000 is the more realistic range for Lean FIRE.

faqs.question: How do I start Lean FIRE planning?
faqs.answer: Lean FIRE planning starts with three steps: (1) Track your real spending for 90 days to find your honest baseline. (2) Multiply your annual spend by 25 (4% rule) or 31 (3.25% conservative rule) to get your portfolio target. (3) Calculate how many years at your current savings rate to reach that number.
